At School level, the trigonometrical graphs of y = sin θ\mathbf{\theta}θ ; y = cos θ\mathbf{\theta}θ and y = tan θ\mathbf{\theta}θ are basicall drawn over the domain 0∘\circ∘ ≤θ≤\leq \mathbf{\theta} \leq≤θ≤ 360∘\circ∘, initially over 0∘\circ∘ ≤θ≤\leq \mathbf{\theta} \leq≤θ≤ 90∘\circ∘.

y = sin θ\mathbf{\theta}θ starts at (0o0^{o}0o; 0) and reaches up through (30o ;0,5)0^{o}\ ;0,5)0o ;0,5)to ( 9000^{0}00 ; 1) where it turns.

y = cos θ\mathbf{\theta}θ starts at (0o0^{o}0o;1) and reaches down thru’ (60o ;0,5) t0^{o}\ ;0,5)\ t0o ;0,5) to ( 900;0)0^{0};0)00;0)) where it twists.

y = tan θ\mathbf{\theta}θ starts at (0o0^{o}0o;0) & passes through (45o5^{o}5o:1) striving for infinity at 9000^{0}00 .

When we draw all 3 on the same axes from 0 degrees to 360 degrees, we get:

But there is something HORRID – Really horrid! What? …. Why? ……

It ‘seems’ that sin θ (\theta\ (θ (In green) and tan θ\thetaθ (in red) kiss each other over a whole range of values either side of 0o0^{o}0o. Is this true? What can de done to obviate this?

  • In sketching graphs we should try to be aware that we are more accurate when the angle is not ion degrees but in radians. 360o0^{o}0o = 2π\piπ radians, thus 90o0^{o}0o = π2\frac{\pi}{2}2π​ that approcimates to 1,57….. Thus sin θ\thetaθ goes from (000^{0}00 ; 0) to

( 90o;1)0^{o};1)0o;1) that is be ( π2\frac{\pi}{2}2π​ ; 1) in radians, seen as seen roughly as ( 1,57 ; 1). Two Real numbers that can be kept to scale, especially when we sketch graphs.
A dilemma occurs if one draws them on the same axes. It ‘seems’ that sin x and tan x kiss each other over a whole range of values either side of 0o\mathbf{0}^{\mathbf{o}}0o. Is this true?

At what x values, over the domain 0∘\mathbf{\circ}∘ ≤x≤\mathbf{\leq x \leq}≤x≤ 360∘\mathbf{\circ}∘ is sin θ\mathbf{\theta}θ = tan θ ?\mathbf{\theta\ ?}θ ?

This will be laid out on the next page Solutions Question 1, revealing that THE ONLY INTERSECTIONS are at 0∘\circ∘; 180∘\circ∘; 360∘\circ∘.

The drawing of their graphs should reflect this – in fact they MUST - and nothing else. What do we use so as to be accurate? But will this accurate sketch impress or confuse the reader. Verbalise the problem.

We could do a point-by-point plotting of y = sin θ and y=\theta\ and\ y = θ and y= tan θ\thetaθ for angles close to and then moving away from ( 0o0^{o}0o ; 0)

We will try to consider THE RELATIONSHIP between y = sin θ and y=\theta\ and\ y = θ and y= tan θ\thetaθ.

What?

Is there a Relationship between y = sin θ and y=\theta\ and\ y = θ and y= tan θ\thetaθ , initially, for these angles?

What is this RELATIONSHIP between sin θ andtan⁡θ  \theta\ and\tan{\theta\ \ }θ andtanθ   known as?

 x\ x x 0 5∘\mathbf{5\circ}5∘ 8∘\mathbf{8\circ}8∘ 10∘\mathbf{\circ}∘ 12∘\mathbf{\circ}∘ 15∘\mathbf{\circ}∘ 20∘\mathbf{20\circ}20∘ 30∘\mathbf{30\circ}30∘ 45∘\mathbf{\circ}∘
sin⁡x\sin xsinx 0 0,08755 0,1391 0,1736 0,2079 0,2588 0,3420 0,5 0,7071
tan x 0 0,0874 0,1405 0,1763 0,2125 0,2679 0,3639 0,577 1

Matriculants will know that the ratio (Relationship) of sin θ\thetaθ to tan θ\thetaθ as cos . θ\thetaθ

sin⁡θtan⁡θ= sin⁡θsin⁡θ . cos⁡θ1\frac{\sin\theta}{\tan\theta} = \ \frac{\sin\theta}{\sin\theta}\ .\ \frac{\cos\theta}{1}tanθsinθ​= sinθsinθ​ . 1cosθ​ = cos θ\thetaθ that will reveal the percentage proximity of these values.

and thus you will see why the 2 graphs seem to sit on each other, as if they did not cut at a single point. Look at the Percentage of the one to the other - or as a decimal. Compare this proximity of sin θ\thetaθ to tan θ\thetaθ to the cos θ\thetaθ value of that angle

Complete the Cos of each of these angles, and let the penny drop.

Decimal

Percent

0,996

99,6%

 0,990 99%

0,984

98,4%

0,978

97,8%

0,965

96,5%

0,939

93,9%

0.866

86,6%

0,707

70,7%
cos θ\thetaθ 1

  • Verbalise what you have discovered.

Afterthought – the astute Mathematician might argue, that in this context 0 divided by 0 could well be 100%, being the only point in that region that gives 100% accuracy - or is that a trick? What is the ratio of sin θ\mathbf{\theta}θ to tan θ\mathbf{\theta}θ? Can you see this relationship as the CRUX to this Investigation? Think. Deep but in front of you. It will explain why those graphs seemingly run on top of each other.

SOLUTIONS - TESTING THOSE BRAINCELLS TOPIC 170

L`CT

1. Many pupils have y = sin θ\thetaθ cutting y = tan θ\thetaθ between 0∘\circ∘ and 90∘\circ∘ which is Not the case as the calculation will show convincingly 3 x 3 = (9)

2(i) . k ϵ  Z\epsilon\ \ Zϵ  Z ( k is an Integer)

sin θ\mathbf{\theta}θ = cos θ\mathbf{\theta}θ (1st and 2nd Quadrants)

= sin (90∘− θ)+\circ - \ \theta) +∘− θ)+ k 360∘\circ∘ or θ\thetaθ = (180∘\circ∘- (90∘− θ) +k360∘\circ - \ \theta)\ + k360\circ∘− θ) +k360∘

             θ=90∘\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \theta = 90\circ             θ=90∘ - θ\thetaθ + k 360∘\circ∘ or θ\thetaθ = 90∘\circ∘ + θ\thetaθ + k.360∘\circ∘

2θ\thetaθ = 90∘\circ∘ + k 360∘\circ∘ θ∘\theta\circθ∘ = 90∘\circ∘ + k.360∘\circ∘

θ\thetaθ = 45∘\circ∘ + k.180∘\circ∘ →   \rightarrow \ \ \ →    absurd

Hence, as seen on the graph that the 2 graphs cut at { 45∘\circ∘ : 225∘\circ∘} →\rightarrow→

sin θ\thetaθ = tan θ\thetaθ cos θ\thetaθ = tan θ\thetaθ sin θ  \theta\ \ θ  = sin θ cos⁡θ\frac{sin\ \theta}{\ \cos\theta} cosθsin θ​ cos θ\thetaθ = sin θ cos⁡θ\frac{sin\ \theta}{\ \cos\theta} cosθsin θ​

sin θ.cos⁡θ\theta.\cos\thetaθ.cosθ - sin θ\thetaθ = 0 cos⁡2θ\cos^{2}\thetacos2θ = sin θ\thetaθ

sin θ\thetaθ (cos θ\thetaθ - 1 ) = 0 cos⁡2θ\cos^{2}\thetacos2θ - sin θ\thetaθ = 0

sin θ=0   or  cosθ=1\theta = 0\ \ \ or{\ \ cos}{\theta = 1}θ=0   or  cosθ=1 sin2θn^{2}\thetan2θ + sin θ−1\theta - 1θ−1 = 0 Δ\mathrm{\Delta}Δ = 1 – 4(1)(-1) = 5

sin θ\thetaθ = −1 ± 52(1)\frac{- 1\ \pm \ \sqrt{5}}{2(1)}2(1)−1 ± 5​​

 θ=0∘+k180∘\ \theta = 0\circ + k180\circ θ=0∘+k180∘ or θ=k.360∘\theta = k.360\circθ=k.360∘ sin θ =0,6180334\theta\ = 0,6180334θ =0,6180334 (the Golden Ratio !)

Hence θ\thetaθ = k. 180∘\circ∘ →\rightarrow→ θ=31,8∘+k360∘\theta = 31,8\circ + k360\circθ=31,8∘+k360∘ or θ\thetaθ = 148,2∘\circ∘ + k360∘\circ∘

Overall solution is {  31,8∘; 148,2∘\ 31,8\circ;\ 148,2\circ 31,8∘; 148,2∘} →\rightarrow→

Solution is { 0∘  ;180∘ ; 360∘\circ\ \ ;180\circ\ ;\ 360\circ∘  ;180∘ ; 360∘} as shown on the graph 3 x 3 + 2 =(9) + (2)

Question 2. 12 =  1      and   221^{2}\ = \ \ 1\ \ \ \ \ \ and\ \ \ 2^{2}12 =  1      and   22 = 4, so 2  lies between 1 and 2\sqrt{2}\ \ lies\ between\ 1\ and\ 22​  lies between 1 and 2

. 1,42 = 1,96  and   1,{1,4}^{2}\ = \ 1,96\ \ and\ \ \ 1,1,42 = 1,96  and   1, 525^{2}52 = 2,25 so 2  lies between 1,4 and 1,5\sqrt{2}\ \ lies\ between\ 1,4\ and\ 1,52​  lies between 1,4 and 1,5

 1,412 = 1,9881  and   1,{\ 1,41}^{2}\ = \ 1,9881\ \ and\ \ \ 1, 1,412 = 1,9881  and   1, 42242^{2}422 = 2,0164 so 2  lies between 1,41 and 1,42\sqrt{2}\ \ lies\ between\ 1,41\ and\ 1,422​  lies between 1,41 and 1,42

1,4142 = 1,999386  & 1,{1,414}^{2}\ = \ 1,999386\ \ \&\ 1,1,4142 = 1,999386  & 1, 4152415^{2}4152 = 2,002225 so 2  lies between 1,414 and 1,415\sqrt{2}\ \ lies\ between\ 1,414\ and\ 1,4152​  lies between 1,414 and 1,415

1,41422=1,99996164 & 1,41432{1,4142}^{2} = 1,99996164\ \&\ 1,4143^{2}1,41422=1,99996164 & 1,41432 = 2,00024449 so x ϵ\epsilonϵ( 1,4142 : 1,4143)

1,414212=1,999989924 & 1,414222{1,41421}^{2} = 1,999989924\ \&\ 1,41422^{2}1,414212=1,999989924 & 1,414222 = 2,000018208 so x ϵ\epsilonϵ( 1,41421 : 1,41422)

Thus 2  ≈\sqrt{2}\ \ \approx2​  ≈ 1,4142 … →\rightarrow→ (2)

Question 2(ii) (2 )2  =  2     \sqrt{2}\ )^{2}\ \ = \ \ 2\ \ \ \ \ 2​ )2  =  2      compared to 1,414212 =  1, 999989924{1,41421}^{2}\ = \ \ 1,\ 9999899241,414212 =  1, 999989924

1,4142135622=2^{2} = 22= 2,000084853 (1)

Numbers determined on the calculator go a few more places into the memory.

Question 3: For 64 players, there are 63 losers and each game is played so as to

get a loser. Hence 63 games played. (1)

3(ii) . 64→32 → 16 → 8→4→2 →1  (1)64 \rightarrow 32\ \rightarrow \ 16\ \rightarrow \ 8 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 2\ \rightarrow 1\ \ (1)64→32 → 16 → 8→4→2 →1  (1) WINNER PLAYS 6 matches (262^{6}26 = 64)

Question 4:

14\frac{1}{4}41​ ( Square – Circle) = 14\frac{1}{4}41​ (100 - π 52\pi\ 5^{2}π 52) = 14\frac{1}{4}41​ ( 21,46018366 .. ) = 5,365045915 . (3)

Question 5:

1234567893 x 1234567894 – 1234567895 x 1234567892

let the smallest 1234567892 = x

( x + 1) ( x + 2) - (x + 3) x

x2x^{2}x2 + 3x + 2 - x2x^{2}x2 - 3 x = 2 →\rightarrow→ (2)

Question 6:

(a) (x – 1 )2  = x2)^{2}\ \ = \ x^{2})2  = x2 - 1 (b) (x – 1 )3  = x3)^{3}\ \ = \ x^{3})3  = x3 – 1

x2 −2x +  1 =  x2−1x^{2}\ - 2x\ + \ \ 1\ = \ \ x^{2} - 1x2 −2x +  1 =  x2−1 x3−3x2 +  3x −1x^{3} - 3x^{2}\ + \ \ 3x\ - 1x3−3x2 +  3x −1 = x3x^{3}x3 – 1

- 2x = -2 −3x2 +  3x- 3x^{2}\ + \ \ 3x−3x2 +  3x = 0

x = 1 →\rightarrow→ (1) -3x ( x – 1) = 0

x = 0 or x = 1 →\rightarrow→ (2)

(c) (d) x = x\sqrt{x}x​ →  x ≥\rightarrow \ \ x\ \geq→  x ≥ 0

(x – 1 )4  = x4−1)^{4}\ \ = \ x^{4} - 1)4  = x4−1 Square both sides

1x4x^{4}x4 - 4 x3x^{3}x3 + 6 x2x^{2}x2 - 4x + 1 = x4−1x^{4} - 1x4−1 x2x^{2}x2 = x

- 4 x3x^{3}x3 + 6 x2x^{2}x2 - 4x + 2 = 0 x2x^{2}x2 - x = 0

4 x3x^{3}x3 - 6 x2x^{2}x2 + 4x - 2 = 0 x ( x – 1) = 0

f(1) = 4 - 6 + 4 -2 = 0 x = 0 or x = 1 Both valid (1)

x = 1 is a root

(x – 1) is a factor

(x – 1) ( 4x2x^{2}x2 - 2x + 2) = 0

Δ =(−2)2−4 (4)(2)= 4−32= −28\mathrm{\Delta}\ = ( - 2)^{2} - 4\ (4)(2) = \ 4 - 32 = \ - 28Δ =(−2)2−4 (4)(2)= 4−32= −28

only root is x – 1 (3)

(e):

x2 <  xx^{2}\ < \ \ xx2 <  x ( draw graph of each)

It is seen that y = x2x^{2}x2 is less

than y = x between where the graphs intersect.

0 < x < 1 (2)

 CMB
Question 7:

  1. Semi-Circle radius 2 – 2 semi-circles of radius 1

Area = 12  π\frac{1}{2}\ \ \pi21​  π (2)2)^{2})2 - 2 . 12\ \frac{1}{2} 21​ π (1)2\pi\ ({1)}^{2}π (1)2 = 1 π\piπ

(b)  12  π  (o,5)2  12  π  (1)2 \frac{\ \frac{1}{2}\ \ \pi\ \ (o,5)^{2}\ }{\ \frac{1}{2}\ \ \pi\ \ (1)^{2}\ } 21​  π  (1)2  21​  π  (o,5)2 ​ = 0,25 (c)  12  π  (o,5)2  12  π  (4)2 \frac{\ \frac{1}{2}\ \ \pi\ \ (o,5)^{2}\ }{\ \frac{1}{2}\ \ \pi\ \ (4)^{2}\ } 21​  π  (4)2  21​  π  (o,5)2 ​ = 0,2516\frac{0,25}{16}160,25​ = 164\frac{1}{64}641​

(d) Half of Circumference = π r\pi\ rπ r

                1π 4 +  2 π  2+   4  π 1 +  8   π  (0,5)  π \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 1\pi\ 4\ + \ \ 2\ \pi\ \ 2 + \ \ \ 4\ \ \pi\ 1\ + \ \ 8\ \ \ \pi\ \ (0,5)\ \ \pi\                 1π 4 +  2 π  2+   4  π 1 +  8   π  (0,5)  π  = 16 π\piπ

(note the equal length of each)

Question 8:

14\frac{1}{4}41​ π\piπ (R)2{R)}^{2}R)2 = 16  π  (r)2\frac{1}{6}\ \ \pi\ \ ({r)}^{2}61​  π  (r)2 MULTIPLY BY 12 AND DIVIDE BY π\piπ

3 R2R^{2}R2 = 2 r2r^{2}r2

Hence R2r2  =    23\frac{R^{2}}{r^{2}}\ \ = \ \ \ \ \frac{2}{3}r2R2​  =    32​

Hence Radiusradius\frac{Radius}{radius}radiusRadius​ = 23\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}3​2​​ or 63\frac{\sqrt{6}}{3}36​​

In   y= ax2+bx+c\ \ y = \ ax^{2} + bx + c  y= ax2+bx+c, maximum is where x = −b2a\frac{- b}{2a}2a−b​ = −3002 (−2)\frac{- 300}{2\ ( - 2)}2 (−2)−300​ = 75, and maximum profit

is P(75) = - 2 (75)2)^{2})2 + 600 (75) +1000 = -11250 + 45000 + 1000 = R44750

Volume = 13  π (3)2 h\frac{1}{3}\ \ {\pi\ (3)}^{2}\ h31​  π (3)2 h = = 13  π (9)1 10\frac{1}{3}\ \ {\pi\ (9)}^{1}\ 1031​  π (9)1 10 = 30 π \pi\ π 

13  π (r)2 5\frac{1}{3}\ \ {\pi\ (r)}^{2}\ 531​  π (r)2 5 = = 13  π (3)2 10\frac{1}{3}\ \ {\pi\ (3)}^{2}\ 1031​  π (3)2 10

5 r2r^{2}r2 = 30

                r2{\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ r}^{2}                r2 = 6 Hence radius is 6\sqrt{6}6​ cm →\rightarrow→

Question 11

  1. let x = 6+6+6+ 6+….\sqrt{6 + \sqrt{6 + \sqrt{6 + \ \sqrt{6 + \ldots.}}}}6+6+6+ 6+….​​​​

Thus x = 6+x\sqrt{6 + x}6+x​ (you can SEE this)

Thus x ≥  0\geq \ \ 0≥  0 and 6 + x ≥  0\geq \ \ 0≥  0 Thus x ≥  0\geq \ \ 0≥  0 →\rightarrow→

                       x2=6+x{\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ x}^{2} = 6 + x                       x2=6+x

x2x^{2}x2 - x - 6 = 0

( x + 2) ( - 3) = 0

x = -2 or x =3

Invalid Valid Hence x = 3 →\rightarrow→

* After 10 Root signs, would the solution be 3?

  1. Determine the maximum value of −2x2+8x−1\sqrt{- 2x^{2} + 8x - 1}−2x2+8x−1​

Maximum value of −2x2{- 2x}^{2}−2x2 + 8x -1 is −Δ4a\frac{- \mathrm{\Delta}}{4a}4a−Δ​

Δ\mathrm{\Delta}Δ = (8)2−4 (−2)(−1))^{2} - 4\ ( - 2)( - 1))2−4 (−2)(−1) = 64 – 8 = 56

Maximum value of −2x2{- 2x}^{2}−2x2 + 8x -1 = − Δ4a\frac{- \ \mathrm{\Delta}}{4a}4a− Δ​ = −564(−2)\frac{- 56}{4( - 2)}4(−2)−56​ = 7

Maximum value of −2x2+8x−1\sqrt{- 2x^{2} + 8x - 1}−2x2+8x−1​ is 7\sqrt{7}7​

Alternate: Consider f(x) = −2x2{- 2x}^{2}−2x2 + 8x -1

f&#39;(x) = -4 x + 8 = 0

Maximum value of −2x2{- 2x}^{2}−2x2 + 8x -1 is f( x ) = 2

f(2)=−2(2)2{f(2) = - 2(2)}^{2}f(2)=−2(2)2 + 8(2) -1 = - 8 + 16 – 1 = 7

Maximum value of −2x2+8x−1\sqrt{- 2x^{2} + 8x - 1}−2x2+8x−1​ is 7\sqrt{7}7​ →\rightarrow→

(a) xy = 6 and y = x + 1 (b) (x + 2) ( y – 1) = 0

x(x + 1 ) = 6 x = - 2 for any value of y or y = 1 for any value of x

x2+xx^{2} + xx2+x - 6 = 0

(x + 3)(x – 2) = 0 (c) no solution, an each term is ≥\geq≥ 0

x = -3 or x = 2

y = -2 or y = 3 (d) if x – 2 = 0 AND y + 2 = 0

(-3 ; -2) ; ( (2 ; 3) →\rightarrow→ ( 2 ; -2) →\rightarrow→

14(a)

Draw the graph to see that y = ( 13 )x{(\ \frac{1}{3}\ )}^{x}( 31​ )xis bigger than y = 3x3^{x}3x if x < 0. CMB

14 (b)

to see

k = k\sqrt{k}k​ implies k = 0 or k = 1

x2−4=0x^{2} - 4 = 0x2−4=0 or x2−4=1x^{2} - 4 = 1x2−4=1

x2=x^{2} = x2=4 or x2=5x^{2} = 5x2=5

So x- values at intersections are

- 5\sqrt{5}5​ ; -2; 2 ; 5\sqrt{5}5​

 CMB

14(c)

sin 2x = 2 sin x

2sin x cos x = 2 sin x

2 sin x cos x - 2 sin x = 0

2sin x ( cos x – 1) = 0

sin x = 0 or cos x = 1

(from graphs, we see)

x = k.180∘\circ∘ or x = k 360∘\circ∘

hence x = k.180 ∘\circ∘, k ϵ\epsilonϵ Z .

Obviously, these 2 red graphs are not ‘accurate’, because of the thickness of the pen, as discussed earlier.

 CMB
Caleb Bell (CMB) used his architectural talent to draw many of the Graphics in this book, once my son Laurence could not continue. Later he was supported by his brother Luke and friends. In Topic 42 you will see part of the portfolio Caleb tendered for his degree in Architecture at U.C.T. (2019). At the end of Topic 49 is a lamp structured by the Fibonacci Series (the Golden Ratio). His next ambition is to complete his Masters. Operating in London.


MAKING A GAME AND HAVING A PICNIC TOPIC 171

A Suggested Maths Play Day – suitable to Grade 8, Grade 9. Choose a day at the end of a term where Senior classes are writing formal examinations. Enlist 4 or 5 mothers – especially those who might be a bit too critical of you. The excited pupils arrive with cooldrinks and picnic food. Accumulate some prizes. Spread yourselves around the Tennis Courts. I have made 5 worksheets below - A; B; C; D; E, and a sketch sheet. Copies are placed on a table with suitable stones to prevent wind interruption. Venue – a tennis court, or a hall in winter.

Each copy has a list of questions (with marks indicated) typed out with space provided for the Group’s reply. Form groups of 5 pupils each, varying the ability. They can be named after famous Mathematicians – Euler, Gauss, Ramanujan, Pythagoras, Lovelace. Prepare banners and logos and research in advance. Choose an historic hero.

Tell them there will be a special prize donated by a Mother, but do not tell them that it is for the winning team. Give it – later - to who gave others in their team a role - the apparent ‘weaker pupil’ - and made him /her feel belonged.

The teams can meet beforehand and strategize tactics. Watch for the one who outreaches as opposed to the one who is gifted. Different strategies will emerge.

The team can take any of the 5 worksheets – even more than 1 – at any time. They are using Their OWN tactics. Do not prescribe. No Calculator may be used.

When a Worksheet is completed, with the team’s name thereon, it is rushed to the top table and given to one of the 5 mothers to mark according to the memorandum, with the teacher as arbitrator, having personalised the awarding of marks. Keep score. At a certain time, a whistle blows. Cooldrinks, sweets, chocolate, cake, music, sausage rolls follow whilst the final tally is done to announce the top group, but mainly, the most humane group and/or the most humane leader who involved others. Do not interfere with the way pupils want to operate. Do not scowl.

* Prepare cut-outs for Worksheet 3. There are 5 Worksheets:

Reference Topics Level Points Earned
Worksheet A Light and Easy Arithmetic and Algebra 15
Worksheet B Routine Processing Algebra 18
Worksheet C Shape Spatial Awareness 12
Worksheet D Thinking Out of the Box 21
Worksheet E Project The Racing Bike 14
TOTAL Score Out of 80 →\rightarrow→

Your Group (circle) : A B C D E F

Your Team : 1. …………………………… 2 ………….………..

3. ………………………………4……………………

5. ………………………………6……………………

Your Team’s Nomination as Best Team Person : …………………………

Follow up with a 40 minute answer session for all, or a hand-out of the memo.

Page of Sketches: Hand out to each group HANDOUT 1 OF 2

Worksheet A6:

(a) Given a 4 x 4 Square.

Worksheet C1:

Given 4 different triangles, suitably annotated

(a)

Right angled Triangle, with ACB = 90∘\circ∘. BC = AC = 3

(b)

Right-angled Triangle, with DEF = 90∘\circ∘. DE = 3 ; EF = 4 and DF = 5.

(c)

Isosceles triangle with sides GH = GK = 4 and AGK = 36∘\circ∘

(d)

Scalene triangle MNL, with NL = 6 and LM = 4 and MN = 5

Worksheet E HANDOUT 2 of 2. - Find a pupil who ‘knows bikes’ or ‘races’ .

Winner is team that gave the best explanation on how a bike works and the role of the parts.

Image 497

 Figure 1: In the 19th Century, we had mules in the farmland, donkeys pulling the produce trucks, horses used in transportation and in providing ‘gentlemen’s sports’ – like racing. The Napoleonic Wars reduced the horse population whilst the ash from an Indonesian volcano in 1815 ruined crops that lay in sodden mud. An alternative was sought. In 1817, the 28 year old Baron Karl von Drais put together the ‘Draisine’ – 2 wheels, a seat, a steering wheel, from wood weighing 23 kilogram. Later 3 wheels and 4 wheels were added. It was propelled by Leg power.
Figure 2: Circa 1870, the Penny Farthing was front driven, with a decided Mechanical Advantage, that increased as the diameter increased. It was great fun – more amusing than functional. There were 2 reasons why it was short-lived. Why? Image 498

Figure 3: One of the reasons was that it was unbecoming – and impossible – for women to ride, because of the clothing they wore at the time. The challenge was to accommodate women – once the bike was made more safely.

Image 499
Figure 4 Image 500 Figure 4: J.K.Starley of Coventry, in the 1890s devised the Rover Safety Bike, where the wheels were of equal size, and where spokes acted as tangents to the road. The seat was over the rear wheel. Later there was a return to bigger wheels at the front as on the right. Figure 5 Image 501

And then the emphasis moved to the cogs.

Figure 6 Image 502

Figure 7 Image 503

Figure 8 Image 504

Worksheet A: TEAM : MARKS OUT OF 15

  • No Calculator may be used.

A1: Determine 272^{7}27 - 727^{2}72 …………………………….(1)

A2: Two numbers differ by 13. One is 42.

Write down the number(s) involved. ………………………….….(2)

A3: If 24 men build 15 walls in 6 days, and all other factors are equal,

How long will it take 18 men to build 18 walls? ……………………………...(2)

A4: As I was going to St Ives, I met a man with 7 wives.

7 wives had 7 cats. 7 cats had 7 rats.

How many were going to St Ives? …………………………….(1)

A5: Who killed Cock Robin? ……………………….… (1)

A6: Look at the 4 x 4 Square on the Sketch sheet.

How many squares are there in this figure? …………………………...……….(2)

A7: Simplify the following algebraic expression:

3p + 8 – 2p – p + 5 + p - 5p ……………..……………..(1)

A8: Simplify given that m = - 3 and n = 4

4m - 2n – 8mn …………….………….…...(2)

A9: Which is bigger and by how much? 67 x 98 or 76 x 89 ?

……………………………………………………………………………………...….…….(2)

A10: If the United States of America sent a modern craft into space in a straight path for exactly 3 years, STATE (You are not expected to calculate) how long in time it would take light to travel that same distance. For your information, take it that light travels at the speed of 300 000 000 kilometres per second. No calculation is necessary – just propose ‘a good guess’. Would it be 12 hours, 12 seconds or 0,12 seconds?

It is one of these answers. (1)

Image 505

Handed in by GROUP ……………………. who scored ……….. out of 15.

Worksheet B: TEAM : MARKS OUT OF 18

  • No Calculator may be used.

The first 3 questions are Multiple Choice: Write down the symbol indicating the correct solution:

B1: Which of the following is an incorrect simplfication of 3x – x + 5x ?

(a) 7 + x (b) 7. x (c) 7x (d) 8 x – 1x …………… (1)

B2: The value of xxx^{x}xx + x2x^{2}x2 - x if x = 3 is equal to

(a) 5 (b) 17 (c) 33 (d) 14 ……………. .(1)

B3: The solution of the equation x2x^{2}x2 = 25 is

(a) - 5 (b) 5 (c) 12,5 (d) ±\pm± 5 ……………..(2)

B4: If P = 3x2x^{2}x2- 2x -5 and Q = x2x^{2}x2 - 2x + 1 determine, in terms of x, the expression

for (a) P – Q ..………………………………………………………………. (2)

and (b) 2 P – 3Q …..……………………………………………………………………….……………...(3)

B5: Simplify : - 3 - ( - 2) - 3 (-2) …………………………………………….… (2)

B6: If x2  − y2  =  (x+y )( x−y)x^{2}\ \ - \ y^{2}\ \ = \ \ (x + y\ )(\ x - y)x2  − y2  =  (x+y )( x−y), determine the value of 532− 47253^{2} - \ 47^{2}532− 472

………………………………………………………………………………….. (2)

B7: Determine the value of

  1. 12 + 13\frac{1}{2}\ + \ \frac{1}{3}21​ + 31​ x  35\ \frac{3}{5} 53​ ………………………………………………………………. (2)

(b) 1, 75 - 32\frac{3}{2}23​ + 50 % ……………………………………………………….... (1)

B8: If in an initial test you got 40 %, and in a following test you got 80 %, your improvement has been (i) 40% (ii) 100%. State the correct answer, with comment:

………………………………………………………………………………………..... (2)

Image 506

MARKS FOR THIS PAGE : 18 GROUP NAME :

Worksheet C: TEAM : MARKS OUT OF 12

  • No Calculator May Be Used

On the Workheet, you are given 4 triangles

(a) a Right- angled triangle ABC with the 2 smaller sides, BC = AC = 3 cm.

  1. a Right angled triangle DEF with sides DE =3 cm, EF = 4cm , DF = 5 cm.

  2. an isosceles triangle GHK with GH = GK = 4 cm and angle G = 36∘\circ∘ .

  3. a Triangle LMN with no sides equal. LM = 4 cm, MN = 5cm and LN = 6cm.

Either by tracing or by drawing and labelling, show if you can make each of these shapes mentioned below by combining any of the 4 given triangles. You can use the same figure more than once. (Your drawing does not have to be to scale but must be labelled). On each side, write its length. We just want to see your ideas.

2 marks for each successful ‘answer’ . Only 1 answer per question.

A Rectangle that is not a Square A Square A Rhombus that is not a Square
A Parallelogram that is not a Rectangle nor a Rhombus A Quadrilateral that cannot be described as any other figure A Kite that is not a Square nor a Rhombus

6 x 2 = 12 GROUP : MARK OUT OF 12:

Worksheet D: TEAM : MARKS OUT OF 21

* No Calculator May Be Used

D1:The volume of a square-based pyramid is given by the formula V = 13\frac{1}{3}31​ Area of Base times Perpendicular height. The base ABCD has equal sides of 10 cm and the perpendicular height PM is 9 cm. You then decide to halve each side and to halve the perpendicular height to make a smaller pyramid. How many of these smaller pyramids would be needed to collectively hold the same volume that the big pyramid held? …….……………………………………………………………………….……..……….(2)

D2: You stand 10 metres in front of a flat plane (normal) mirror. If you ran away from the mirror at 5 km per hour, at what speed would you be running from your image ? …………………………………………………………………………………………….(2)

D3: You have 3 Blue pens, 2 Red pens and 5 Purple pens in a pouch.

  1. What is the probabilty that you would pull out a Purple, a Red, then a Blue and finally another Purple in order without putting them back in the box each time ……… …………………………………………………………(2)

  2. This time, you do return the used pen to the box. What is the probability that you would pull out a Purple, a Red, then a Blue and finally another Purple in order? ……………………… …………………………….……(2)

D4: First a little story:

Sir Isaac Newton, in the 16th century, regarded the concepts of mass, distance and time (and hence distance per time that is speed - or velocity - and velocity per second (that is acceleration) as ABSOLUTE concepts. The full, final and unchangeable explanations of the universe! He stated 3 laws. An object would continue in a static position, or continue moving until it is influenced by another force. For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. His 3rd law measured Force as Mass times Acceleration, written as F = ma. In effect he defined FORCE in terms of the 3 ‘absolutes’ that – to him – governed the heavenly bodies: mass, distance, time. This led to the Mechanical Age and The Industrial Revolution - that was to see Britain covered with factories and ships sailing the seas.

In the 1870s the research of Michael Faraday and James Maxwell taught man about electricity and its link to magnetism. The phenomenon of electro-magnetism led to the development of cars and even more factory workshops and aeroplanes. Maxwell spoke of The Indestructibility of Matter, and determined that electro-magnetic waves travel at what we now call ‘the speed of light’ – very close to 300 x 10610^{6}106 kilometres per second. Over 7 times round the earth in a second! Albert Einstein, in 1905, in a hurry, wrote down E = mc2c^{2}c2, where he regarded these very concepts not as Absolutes but as RELATIVE and spoke of The Indestructibility of Energy to add to Maxwell’s Indestructibility of Mass. He showed the link between Energy and Mass. One would change to the other.

For the astute amongst you, he meant to write EnergyMass\frac{Energy}{Mass}MassEnergy​ = (speed of light)2)^{2})2 but he was in a hurry and wrote E = mc2c^{2}c2, making it tougher to understand what he really meant. If each of Energy and Mass were indestructible, then when we ‘see’ mass being apparently destroyed, it is ‘energy’ being released. This was the idea of the atomic Bomb, and of the creation of galaxies – the life of stars.

And ‘mass’ would form if ‘energy’ seemed to be destroyed – what happens in the life of stars and galaxies.

See Question D4 on this page.

Do not be worried about any units at this stage as you will not be expected to change any of what is given.

James Joule worked on electro-magnetism. He died in 1889, more than 300 years after Newton. A Joule is the unit for Work (‘Energy’). Just concentrate on the numbers.

D4 Question: Use this formula E = mc2c^{2}c2, to determine (in kilograms) the mass (as that will be the result) that the Sun emits in 1 second, if it loses energy of 360 x 102410^{24}1024 Joules and you are given that the speed of light is taken as 300 x 10610^{6}106 kilometre per second. ……………………………………………………………………………………..…..…….. (3)

James Prescott Joule Albert Einstein Sir Isaac Newton

Image 507 Image 508 Image 509

A Newton is the Unit of Force. It is the force applied if a Mass of 1 Kilogram accelerates at the rate of 1 metre per second per second.

A Joule is the measure of Energy (or Work Done) when a force of 1 Newton moves a distance of 1 metre.

A Watt is the Power extended when Work of 1 Joule is done in a time of 1 second. It honours the memory of James Watt (died 1819) who rose from being a near orphan to being the main guy behind the Steam engine, that linked British cities – and brought loads of soot to so many towns. The Carbon footprint.

D5: Suppose that in your class are 10 boys and 10 girls. The Headmaster can call in on his cellphone, saying “Send me a child”.

  1. After how many calls can he know for sure that he will have 2 of the

same gender? ………………………………………………………….(2)

  1. After how many calls, can he be certain that he will have a boy?

………………………………………………………………….……..(2)

D6: Mention a geometric property that a Rhombus has that a Rectangle does not.

…………………………………………………………………………… (2)

D7: Suppose you are given a test with 20 questions. For each question you get correct, you earn 3 points. For each that you get wrong or do not answer, you lose a point. Determine:

(i) the highest score you can earn. ……………………………………………... (1)

(ii) the lowest score you can earn ………………………………………….…….. (1)

(iii) the number you must get correct so as to score ZERO. …………….…….. (2)

E1: If you go into a Sports shop selling bicycles would you see any ‘vehicle’ looking like the Draisine, (see Worksheet photograph) and working on the same propulsion method? What is this method? ……………………………………………………………………………………………(1) ……………………………………………………………………………………………(1)

E2: If Mechanical Advantage = Force one gets outForce put in\frac{Force\ one\ gets\ out}{Force\ put\ in}Force put inForce one gets out​ = Circumference of big front tyreCircumference of….WHAT ?….\frac{Circumference\ of\ big\ front\ tyre}{Circumference\ of\ldots.WHAT\ ?\ldots.}Circumference of….WHAT ?….Circumference of big front tyre​

What do I mean by “WHAT” in this formula? ……………………………………………………………………………………………(1)
E3: Name 2 significant changes that led to the Penny Farthing – the Bone Cruncher – being replaced – leading to the development of the bicycle. (1) + (1) ……………………………………………………………………………………………(1) ……………………………………………………………………………………………(1)
E4: What obstacles are encountered by the technician who wants to develop a bike that can win races like the Tour de France with uphill, downhill and sprinting. ……………………………………………………………………………………………(1) ……………………………………………………………………………………………(1)
E5: Mention a way to solve each of the issues in Question E4 that you identified: …………………………………………………………………………………………....(1) …………………………………………………………………………………………....(1)
E6: Those sprockets tell a story. The rider can – at the expense of speed – change gears in going uphill. Would he choose a smaller radius cog at the back as at the front, or the smaller radius at the front? Justify. ………………………………………………………………………………………...….(1) …………………………………………………………………………………………....(1)

E7: If a racer stood up on his pedals going up a steep hill, his posture would improve, utilising aerodynamics. What part(s) of his body is likely to endure the most pain and/or damage? …………………………………………………………….. ...

…………………………………………………………………………………………..(1) + (1)

E8: A modern racing bike can be 10 speed or 18 speed depending on the combinations between the sprockets at the front and those at the back. At the front, there could be 53 to 50 teeth on the outside and 38 to 26 on the inside. The cyclist selects – controlling from the handlebars. At the back are size 9 to size 32. For a high speed he might choose 53 in the front and 9 at the back. Determine this ratio, to the nearest 2 places, without using the calculator. ………………………….………………………………………………………………. (1)

SOLUTIONS MAKING A GAME & HAVING A PICNIC TOPIC 171

Worksheet A: 15 Hard-earned Marks

A1 : 64 – 49 = 15 (1) A2: 42 – 13 = 29 (1) and 42 + 13 = 55 (1)

A3: Ask “More” or “ Less” each time to grasp the logical sense:

Days taken = 61\frac{6}{1}16​ x 2418\frac{24}{18}1824​ x 1815\frac{18}{15}1518​ = 485\frac{48}{5}548​ = 9,6 days ≈\approx≈ 10 days (2)

A4: One person, being ‘I’, There is no clear evidence that the man was going in my direction. (1) A5: The Sparrow. (1)

A6: One 4 x 4. Four 3 x 3. Nine 2 x 2 . Sixteen 1 x 1. Total: is 30 (2)

A7: - 4p + 13 (1) A8: 4(-3) – 2 (4) – 8(-3)(4) = - 12 - 8 + 96 = 76 (2)

A9: 67 (100 – 2) = 6700 – 134 = 6566

Versus 76 (100 – 10 – 1) = 7600 - 760 - 76 = 7600 - 836 = 7600 – 1000 + 164

= 7764 – 1000 = 6764, which is bigger by 6764 – 6566 = 200 – 2 = 198 (2)

A10: 0,12 Seconds (1)

Worksheet B : 18 marks

B1: 7 + x that is (a) (1) B2: 333^{3}33 + 323^{2}32 - 3 = 27 + 9 – 3 = 33 that is (c) (1)

B3: (d) . (5)2  =25   and  (−5)2{5)}^{2}\ \ = 25\ \ \ and\ \ ({- 5)}^{2}5)2  =25   and  (−5)2 = 25 (2)

B4: (3x2{(3x}^{2}(3x2- 2x - 5) - (x2x^{2}x2 - 2x + 1) = 2x22x^{2}2x2 - 6 (2)

B4: 2(3x2{2(3x}^{2}2(3x2- 2x – 5)-3 (x2x^{2}x2 - 2x+ 1) = 6x2 −4x−10−3 x2+6x6x^{2\ } - 4x - 10 - 3\ x^{2} + 6x6x2 −4x−10−3 x2+6x - 3 = 3x2x^{2}x2 + 2x – 13 (3)

B5: - 3 + 2 + 6 = - 3 + 8 = 5 (2)

B6: (53 + 47 ) ( 53 – 47) = (100). (6) = 600 . No Calculator. (2)

B7: 12 + 13\frac{1}{2}\ + \ \frac{1}{3}21​ + 31​ x 35\frac{3}{5}53​ =  12 + 15\ \ \frac{1}{2}\ + \ \frac{1}{5}  21​ + 51​ = 5+210\frac{5 + 2}{10}105+2​ = 710\frac{7}{10}107​ (2)

B7: 1,75 – 1,50 + 0,50 = 0,75 or 75% or 34\frac{3}{4}43​ (1)

B8: You were not given ‘the difference’ but the increase.

40% of 40% is 16%. So the first scenario is 40% + 16% = 56%. (1)

100% of 40% = 40%. So the new mark would be 40% + 40% = 80% (1)

Many people do not realise that 100% is 1.

Worksheet C : 6 x 2 = 12 marks

Go by what pupil has offered. Just one solution per question.

Pupils might rotate 90∘\circ∘, or 180∘\circ∘ , reflect, or translate. Easy way to mark is if they put equal sides on to each other.

(i) To form a Rectangle:

Reflect Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔDEF across the side DF, and rotate it 90∘\circ∘. Accept their answer, eg, 2 squares could be put alongside each other.

(ii) To form a Square:

Reflect Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔABC across the side AB.

One can rotate the triangle fully around point A)

(iii) To Form a Rhombus.

There are a number of interesting options.

Especially to rotate Δ DEF\mathrm{\Delta}\ DEFΔ DEF around E. There will are 4 components.

(iv) To Form a Parallelogram.

In Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔGHK across any of its 3 sides

Rotate Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔDEF or Δ LMN\mathrm{\Delta}\ LMNΔ LMN right around point E, and then rotate.

(v) To Form a Quadrilateral

On DE of Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔDEF, place AB or AC.

On EF of Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔDEF, place GH or GK of Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔGHK.

On LM of Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔLMN, placed any side equal to 4 from Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔDEF or Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔGHK.

Place DE of Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔDEF on to MN of Δ\mathrm{\Delta}Δ LMN, where each is 5cm.

(vi) To Form a Kite.

Reflect Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔDEF across the side DF.

One can put Δ ABC\mathrm{\Delta}\ ABCΔ ABC (the AB or AC) on to DE of Δ\mathrm{\Delta}ΔDEF, to form a larger triangle,

eg Δ\mathrm{\Delta}Δ DFA and then reflect this across DA.

Reflect Δ\mathrm{\Delta}Δ LMN across any one of its sides

Worksheet D: Maximum : 21

D1: Volume of Initial pyramid = 13\frac{1}{3}31​ x2hx^{2}hx2h = 9003\frac{900}{3}3900​ = 300 cm3{cm}^{3}cm3. Dimensions of small pyramid is 13 . 102 . 102 . 92 = 9003  x 8\frac{1}{3}\ .\ \frac{10}{2}\ .\ \frac{10}{2}\ .\ \frac{9}{2}\ = \ \frac{900}{3\ \ x\ 8}31​ . 210​ . 210​ . 29​ = 3  x 8900​ cm3.{cm}^{3}.cm3. Now, if you made 8 little pyramids and could pack them` into the big one, the simple answer would be 8. But if it was a cone that we used, there would be a lot of space between the 8 little ones that would definitely be more than the original.This answer is thus 8.
D2: The image would move the same distance back as you do. Distance between you and your image doubles in same time, so speed doubles to 10 km/hr (2)

D3(i) : 510 . 29  . 38  . 47   =  142\frac{5}{10}\ .\ \frac{2}{9}\ \ .\ \frac{3}{8}\ \ .\ \frac{4}{7}\ \ \ = \ \ \frac{1}{42}105​ . 92​  . 83​  . 74​   =  421​ (2)

D3 (ii) 510 . 210  . 310  . 410   =  121000\ \frac{5}{10}\ .\ \frac{2}{10}\ \ .\ \frac{3}{10}\ \ .\ \frac{4}{10}\ \ \ = \ \ \frac{12}{1000} 105​ . 102​  . 103​  . 104​   =  100012​ = 0,012 or 3250\frac{3}{250}2503​ (2)

D4: If E = mc2c^{2}c2 then m = EC2\frac{E}{C^{2}}C2E​ = 360    .   1024(300  .  106}2\frac{360\ \ \ \ .\ \ \ 10^{24}}{{(300\ \ .\ \ 10^{6}\}}^{2}}(300  .  106}2360    .   1024​ = 360   .  102490000.   .  1012\frac{360\ \ \ .\ \ 10^{24}}{90000.\ \ \ .\ \ 10^{12}}90000.   .  1012360   .  1024​ = 4 . 10910^{9}109 = 4 trillion kilogram. Wow, this is huge! (3)

* I tried to talk all sorts of blurb to see if you would be put off. Keep calm.

D5. (i) The first could be M or F. The second could be MM, FF or MF or FM. If the next was male, it could be MMM, MMF, MFM. But if it was female, would be MMF, FFF, MFF. In each of these 6 situations there are at least 2 likes. Solution is 3. (2)

(ii). The 10 girls could come out in succession, so he will only be certain after 11

calls that one at least is a boy. (2)

D6: Diagonals bisect each other at 90∘\circ∘. Or. Diagonal bisects the angle from which

it comes. Or. 2 consecutive sides are equal Any one for (2).

D7: Let number correct be ‘c’ and number wrong be ‘w’. Points earned are given as seen 3c – 1 w. But there are 10 questions, so c + w = 20 meaning that w = 20 – c.

Thus points scored are 3c – 1 (20 – c) = 3c – 20 + c = 4c – 20.

(a) Highest is 20(3) = 60 or 4 (20) – 20 = 60 (1)

(b) Lowest is 20 ( -1) = -20 or 4(0) – 20 = -20 (1)

(c) To get zero, 4c – 20 = 0, meaning m = 5. So if you got 5 right you score zero. (2)

How do you know that in a skills contest, the judges do or do not use a plus and a minus method of assessing competitors? This makes sense.
Solutions to Worksheet E2 14 marks
E1: Previously when 2 wheel cycles were produced, they had supporting side wheels – but these ‘hurt’ if the child fell off. You will find ‘2 wheels, a seat and a steering rod, where the 2 year old propels by walking the cycle or free-wheeling on a downslope – the same way of propelling the Draisine. (1) for Young kid’s bike (1) for ‘Walking it!’
E2: Not the Circumference of the back wheel, that is only a support, but the circumference travelled by the foot that is pedalling. One small turn as input resulted in one big turn of the wheel whose diameter was determined by the rider – especially if he was tall. (1)
E3: Any 2 of position of seat; introduction of chain; driving off the back wheel, wheels of equal size or nearly equal. (2)
E4: Any two of weight of the rider; posture of the rider; weight of the bicycle; resistance of the wind (aerodynamics); surface of the road or path. Metal from which the bike is made. Thickness of the wheels. 2 x (1) = 2
E5: Whatever applies, like Diet or gymnastic courses that focus on select parts of the human body. Replacing steel with asbestos or carbon-fibre. Thinness of tyres, etc. (2)
E6: Smaller at back. M A = Force one gets outForce put in\frac{Force\ one\ gets\ out}{Force\ put\ in}Force put inForce one gets out​ = Circumference of big front cogCircumference of back cog\frac{Circumference\ of\ big\ front\ cog}{Circumference\ of\ back\ cog}Circumference of back cogCircumference of big front cog​ (1)
E7: The knees. (Students might have a different opinion). (1)
E8: 9 ) 553,80808085^{5}{3,}^{8}0^{8}0^{8}0^{8}553,8080808 = 5,8888 ≈\approx≈ 5,89 (1)

Leading Cyclists

The Afrikaans name for a petrol-driven cycle was a Bromponie. Brm … brm … brm.

A suitable name for a bicycle could be a “Baie Sukkel”, although a more formal name was “Einspoor” using ‘Ein’ as the Germanic ONE, and Spoor for ‘track’.

Perhaps the greatest South African cyclist, is Rudolph Lewis (1887 – 1933). From Germistion, he worked full-time as a gold-miner. Gold Medalist in the 1912 Olympics, he moved to Germany continuing cycling and winning an Iron Cross.

Ashleigh Moolman – Pasio (born 1985) married to Carl Pasio of Knysna, but living and riding in Spain, has achieved success on the international stage. She is the first winner of the UCI Cycling & Sports event – a Virtual competition held during the CoVid Lockdown. Took up cycling after meeting fitness-fanatic Carl at University.

Chris Froome (born 1985) went to school at St John’s in Johannesburg and studied an University of Johannesburg for 2 years. Born in Kenya, and only started cycling aged 13. Turned professional in 2007 in S.A. Qualified to represent Great Britain and won the Tour de France in 2013, 2015, 2016, 2017 - coming 2nd in 2012 and 3rd in 2018.

Extra Possible Questions. GROUP EXERCISE. GROUP. 13 Bonus Points

What part of the body / bike is parallel to the upper leg? ……………………..….. (1)

Why? ……………………………………...(1)

What part of the body is directly above the pedalling foot? ………………………..…(1)

Was this always the case in cycling?

………………………………………..……(1)

When would the cyclist stand upright on his pedals? ………………………………(1)

Why? …………………………………….(1)

If you were going to design a clock that recorded seconds, minutes and hours, state the number of teeth you might have on 3 attached cogs

………….. …………….. ………….(3)

Graphics from Pinterest

Answer these questions about a Bike Casette1.

It is placed on (a) the front ((b) the back wheel. (1)

  1. If you rode on the 50th tooth ring in the front, and the 11th at the back, would your gear ratio be 50:11 or 11:50?

………………………………….(1)

  1. Would you use this setting (i) to ride on the flat (ii) to go uphill?

…………………………………(1)

  1. If you rode on the 32th tooth ring in the front, and the 30th at the back, would you use that to (i) go on a steep downhill (ii) go on a steep uphill?

………………………………..(1)

Credit Points for the Best Group presentation talk on The Bicycle, especially if it was given by a ‘non-Mathematics’ achiever, who brought in a bike.

BLOCKS AND DOTS. - A League Competition Between Chosen Teams TOPIC 172

The Investigation into and the Game of BLOCKS & DOTS could take a whole Play Day if you run a league and then Knock-out competition, with the final being in the School Hall at a PTA meeting, or with the whole school present – or as Inter-Schools! Sponsors can carry the event.
A teacher could print out pages with a number of squares ( e.g. 6 x 6 ) or rectangular grids (e.g. 6 x 4) and then form 8 leagues with 4 in a team (if your class holds 32 pupils). Each pupil plays 2 games against each in that league. Players are rated 1st, 2nd, 3rd or 4th. In another session, all the 1sts play in a knockout to find an overall winner, who could earn a sponsored prize. All the 2nds, the 3rds and the 4ths can play knockouts to find winners. The final game of the 1sts could take place in a school assembly or at a Parents – Teachers meet. Designed by Eduoard Lucas (1842 – 1891), a Mathematics Professor whose fame was that from 1857, he found that that 2127 −12^{127\ } - 12127 −1 was a Prime Number – in fact it stayed as the largest Prime Number for 75 years. This game has also been called Pig-In-A-Hole, Why? Look at the script AFTER a game has been played and track the paths that the Pig could have dug.
Shown here is a 5 x 5 square of ‘Dots’ where Black plays Red. Where would you, as Black draw a line so as to be safe?Players take turns to join 2 dots horizontally or vertically. If you complete a square, put your initial in that block, and then draw the next line.When all dots have been joined, count to see who wins – by the number of blocks closed - and by how much. In this sketch alongside, the game is incomplete. If you were Red and it was your turn, where would you join 2 dots so as not to concede a BOX to Black? How many options are there?

INVESTIGATION – to let you determine how “FORMULAE for patterns that emerge can be formed just by analysing what you see, We first Investigate SQUARE Grids.

3 x 3 and. 4 x 4

∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗

    ∗\ \ \ \ *    ∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗

    ∗\ \ \ \ *    ∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗

    ∗\ \ \ \ *    ∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗  \  

    ∗\ \ \ \ *    ∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗

5 x 5

∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗

6 x 6

∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗

Study the Table below that records (i) the number of dots on each side ( refer to as n); The number of Dots in that starting sketch (iii) the number of lines (each joining 2 dots) that ‘complete’ the game (iv) the number of Boxes in the completed sketch (v) the FORMULA in terms of n for the number of LINES.

(i) (ii) (iii) (iv) The thinking to get the lines
Side (n) Dots Boxes Lines See the relevant sketch above
2 4 1 4 2 across; 2 down
3 9 4 12 Down are 3 columns of 2 lines and Across are 3 rows of 2 lines
4 16 9 24 Down are 4 columns of 3 lines and Across are 4 rows of 3 lines
5 25 16 40 Down are 5 columns of 4 lines and Across are 5 rows of 4 lines
6 36 25 60 Down are 6 columns of 5 lines and Across are 6 rows of 5 lines
n n2n^{2}n2 (n−1)2{(n - 1)}^{2}(n−1)2 2n(n – 1) Down are n columns of (n-1) lines and Across are n rows of (n-1)
10 100 81 180 Down are 10 columns of 9 lines and Across are 10 rows of 9 lines

* In the formula, do you realise why the 2 in 2n(n – 1) exists?

* If the final contest as an 100 x 100 square, there would be 200 x 99 = 19800 lines at 1 per second, it would take 330 minutes – being 5 hours 30 minutes and might just be too boring.

* Let’s now investigate if we had RECTANGLES of different sides. We will take 6 x 10 as an example, where the horizontal (across) lines called “x “ and the vertical lines (down) called “y”. In this example x = 5 and y = 9.

This is a 5 x 9. RECTANGULAR Grid

Let x = 5 being the number of rows.

Let y = 9 being the number of columns

∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗

∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗ ∗*∗    ∗\ \ \ *   ∗

CHALLENGE: Generate a FORMULA in terms of x and y that determines the number

  1. DOTS. (b) BLOCKS. (c) LINES (the little line joining any 2 dots)

When there are 5 horizontal lines (representing x ) and 9 vertical lines (representing y )

being 5 x 9

Counting The Dots

In each of the x rows, there are y columns.

In each of the 5 rows, there are 9 columns

There are xy dots. In this case 5 x 9 = 45. VERBALISE.

Counting the Blocks:

There are 8 blocks in each of the 4 rows. Total is 32. Analogising,

There are ( x – 1) ( y – 1) blocks VERBALISE

Counting the connecting Lines

In each of the 5 horizontal lines, there are 8 short lines; Sub-total 40. and in each of the 9 vertical lines, there are 4 little lines, being 36. Total connecting lines are 40 + 36 = 76.

Analogising to x and y, the number of little lines is

x (y – 1) + y (x – 1) = xy – x + xy – y = 2xy – (x + y). VERBALISE

Check :

If x = 5 and y = 9, Then 2xy – (x + y) = 2 (5) (9) – ( 5 + 9) = 90 - 14 = 76

  • Thus if we have a 50 by 100 grid, there would be 5000 dots, and 4851 blocks* ,

and 2 (50)( 100) – (50 + 100) = 10000 – 5000 = 5000 lines.

  • How long would (i) a 5 x 9 game and

(ii) a 50 x 100 game take if each move takes 5 seconds?

SOLUTION: (i) 76 x 5 ÷\div ÷60. = 6 minutes 20 seconds

(ii) 5000 x 5 ÷\div÷ 60 = 2500 ÷  6 =\div \ \ 6\ = ÷  6 = 416 minutes 40 seconds

= 69 hours 2 minutes and 40 seconds

= 2 days. 21 hours. 2 minutes. And 40 seconds.

Player One: Name & Initial & Score Player Two: Name & Initial & Score

∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗

∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗

∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗

∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗

∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗ ∗\mathbf{*}∗    ∗\mathbf{\ \ \ *}   ∗

WINNER: TEAM OF WINNER:

ON THE WAY HOME IN THE FAMILY CAR TOPIC 173

1. Observe the following 6 random car license numbers

A CA 116 985 B CK 119453 C CK 119453
D Nd 773476 E CA 4263453 E CA 4263453

In each case, then add those digits, and repeat if need be so that you ger a single digit.

Eg In A; 1 + 1 + 6 + 9 + 8 + 5 = 30. And 3 + 0 = 3. Record these Single digits;

A = 3 B = C = D = E = F =

Now divide each of these license numbers by 9 and record the Remainder.

Eg: 116 985 ÷\div÷ 9. = 1 2 9 9 9 8. Remainder 3. Record this Remainder of 3.

Rem A = 3 Rem B = Rem C = Rem D = Rem E = Rem F =

WHAT CONJECTURE ARE YOU PREPARED TO POSTULATE? Could you say:

“if a number is divided by 9, the remainder is the continued sum of its digits”. ?

  • There seems to be a hiccup with License Number F.

  • Can you Rationalize this, or does it negate our Conjecture? Think.

EXPLANATION There are 2 meanings of Division

PARTITION (into equal parts) QUOTITION (Repeated Subtraction)

24 ÷\mathbf{\div}÷ 4 = 24 ÷2  ÷ 2  \mathbf{\div 2\ \ \div \ 2\ \ }÷2  ÷ 2   = 12 ÷ 2  =6\mathbf{\div \ 2\ \ = 6}÷ 2  =6

Each of the equal parts is 6 units

24 – 4 = 20; 20 – 4 = 16; 16 – 4 = 12

12 – 4 = 8 ; 8 – 4 = 4 ; 4 – 4 = 0

six times

So, when we are dividing, say a 6 digit number by 9, we are effectively subtracting – if possible – a number of 900 000’s, or 90 000’s ; or 9 000’s ; or 9 000’s ; or 900’s; or 90’s or 9’s if you can follow that. We are REPEATEDLY subtracting

+ In adding car license numbers , link 8 with 2. ; 7 with 3 ; 6 with 4 ; 5 with another 5.

CAR LICENCES:

If cars in the Cape Peninsula can be CA (Cape Town), or CL, (Stellenbosch) or CY (Bellville) and they must hold exactly 5 digits, and are not allowed to have a Zero; nor a first nor a last digit as 1, how many cars could register, if all possible numbers are taken.

C A,l,Y Not 0;1 Not 0 Not 0 0 to 9 Not 0;1
1 Option 3 options 8 options 9 Options 9 options 9 options 8 options

Hence number of possible numbers is 1 x 3 x 8 x 9 x 9 x 9 x 8 = 3 x 729 x 64 = 729 x 192.

729 (200 – 8) = 145800 - 5832 = 145800 + 168 – 6000 = 139968. →\rightarrow→

What decision needs to be made? Copyright © 2021
